content.text: A connector marketplace is a curated catalog of pre-built integrations that expose third-party services as agent-callable tools. Instead of writing and maintaining API clients for every service your agent needs, you discover, install, and configure a connector that handles authentication, request formatting, and result parsing.
No marketplace is universally best. The right choice depends on protocol fit, auth model, hosting requirements, and whether you need managed OAuth or custom connector support.

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard for connecting AI models to external tools via JSON-RPC over stdio, HTTP, or Server-Sent Events. MCP servers are first-class citizens in Claude Desktop and Claude Code and are the most direct way to extend an agent with external capabilities.
The Smithery registry (smithery.ai) indexes hundreds of community-built MCP servers. You can query it programmatically to discover servers before installing:
Connecting to an MCP server in code involves starting a subprocess, exchanging JSON-RPC messages to list tools, and routing tool_use blocks from Claude back through the server:
The same agent loop pattern applies regardless of which MCP server you connect to — only the server command changes:

Some directories (APIs.guru, RapidAPI, Postman's public API network) publish OpenAPI specs for thousands of services. You can fetch a spec from a marketplace URL and auto-generate Claude tool schemas from the operations it defines:

This approach gives you maximum flexibility — any REST API with an OpenAPI spec becomes a tool set — but you own the auth layer (API keys, OAuth tokens) and must inject them into each request yourself. See the OpenAPI Spec-Based Tools topic for the full dispatcher implementation.
When evaluating a connector marketplace for production use, weigh these factors:
— MCP for protocol-native integration (first-class in Claude Desktop and Claude Code); OpenAPI for REST-first systems; proprietary SDKs when the platform adds managed auth, sandboxing, or HITL.
— understand who holds credentials. MCP servers often run locally (you own the env vars). Composio/Arcade store OAuth tokens on their platform. Zapier stores them in user Zapier accounts.
— production systems need a record of every tool call. Some platforms provide this; others require you to add logging middleware yourself.
— check when each connector was last updated. A Slack connector six months out of date may use deprecated API endpoints.
— can you add your own internal tools alongside marketplace connectors? Composio's
— does the platform expose rate-limit headers or enforce its own caps on top of the underlying API's limits?

Real agents rarely draw from a single marketplace. A common pattern is to combine a curated set of MCP connectors (for internal services) with a managed platform like Composio (for third-party OAuth apps) and custom-built tools:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Tools from three sources: MCP server, Composio, and local custom tools
mcp_tools = load_mcp_tools(github_mcp_proc)          # GitHub via MCP
composio_tools = toolset.get_tools(apps=[App.SLACK])  # Slack via Composio
custom_tools = [search_internal_wiki_schema]          # Internal tool

all_tools = mcp_tools + composio_tools + custom_tools

resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=2048,
    tools=all_tools,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize the last 3 GitHub PRs and post to #engineering."}],
)

# Dispatch each tool_use block to the correct backend
for block in resp.content:
    if block.type != "tool_use":
        continue
    if block.name in {t["name"] for t in mcp_tools}:
        result = call_mcp_tool(github_mcp_proc, block.name, block.input)
    elif block.name in {t["name"] for t in composio_tools}:
        result = toolset.handle_tool_call(block)
    else:
        result = call_custom_tool(block.name, block.input)
Keep tool names unique across sources. Prefix with the source if needed (e.g.
) so the dispatch logic is unambiguous.

Connector schemas can change between versions. A new required parameter in an MCP server upgrade breaks every agent that calls that tool without the parameter. Pin connector versions explicitly and audit schema diffs before upgrading:

Run the audit function on startup (comparing a saved schema snapshot against the live tool list) and fail fast if breaking changes are detected. This surfaces upgrade problems before they silently corrupt agent behavior.

When an agent uses tools from multiple marketplace sources, you need a unified view of what was called, how long it took, and whether it succeeded. Wrap each dispatcher in a logging decorator rather than instrumenting each backend individually:

import time, logging
from functools import wraps

logger = logging.getLogger('tool_dispatch')

def logged_tool_call(source: str, fn):
    """Wrap a tool dispatcher to log every call with timing and outcome."""
    @wraps(fn)
    def wrapper(name: str, args: dict):
        start = time.monotonic()
        try:
            result = fn(name, args)
            elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
            logger.info(
                "tool_call source=%s name=%s elapsed_ms=%.0f status=ok",
                source, name, elapsed * 1000,
            )
            return result
        except Exception as exc:
            elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
            logger.error(
                "tool_call source=%s name=%s elapsed_ms=%.0f status=error error=%s",
                source, name, elapsed * 1000, exc,
            )
            raise
    return wrapper

# Wrap each dispatcher:
call_mcp = logged_tool_call('mcp', call_mcp_tool)
call_composio = logged_tool_call('composio', lambda n, a: toolset.handle_tool_call_by_name(n, a))

Structured log fields (source, name, elapsed_ms, status) let you filter and aggregate across sources in any log management system. Add a trace_id from the outer request context to correlate all tool calls within a single agent turn.
Test the dispatch layer independently of the tool implementations. Mock each backend at the boundary and verify that routing, error handling, and tool-name uniqueness all hold:
for HTTP-based platforms. Never call real endpoints in unit tests.
— a duplicate tool name silently routes calls to the wrong backend. Check it at startup, not at test time.
— unknown tool names should raise immediately rather than silently return empty results.
Connector marketplaces let agents access hundreds of services without writing individual API clients.
MCP is the protocol-native option — open standard, first-class Claude support, auth stays in your environment.
Smithery indexes community MCP servers; query it programmatically to discover and install connectors.
Managed platforms (Composio, Arcade) store OAuth tokens on their platform and expose structured tool schemas.
OpenAPI-based directories give breadth but require you to own the auth layer.
In production, compose tools from multiple sources; keep names unique and dispatch by source.
Evaluate any marketplace on: auth ownership, audit trail, connector freshness, custom connector support, and rate-limit visibility.
